summary: Cross-chapter audit of the cycle-03 manuscript (75,542 prose words, 2,418 prose lines, 6,461 sentences across 18 chapters). PART A (redundancy): The mandatory comparison protocol identified 16 character re-introduction and setting re-description findings, concentrated in the Ch14-Ch17 stretch where Jae-won's watch (4 descriptions in 3 chapters), Tomas's meal (3 near-verbatim itemizations across Ch14-Ch15), and Lena's territorial control (re-stated in every chapter since Ch7) are the worst offenders. Proof-of-baseline analysis flagged the temperature/cold baseline as the most over-proved property: Chapters 10-13 form a four-chapter cluster re-proving 'the building is getting colder' without materially new consequence, and 'thirty-eight degrees' appears four times across Ch15-Ch16. Sentence-opener monotony is significant: 'The' opens 15.7% of all sentences (1,015/6,461), exceeding 7% in all 18 chapters, with Ch18 at 28.9%. Hedge-word density flags 'still' at 321 occurrences (1 per 235 words, 3.4x the 1/800 threshold) and 'already' at 148 occurrences (1 per 510 words, 1.6x threshold). Image density is critically high for structural nouns: 'corridor' (159x), 'blood' (155x), 'terminal' (131x), 'stairwell' (108x). Em-dash density is 1.28% (31/2,418 prose lines) — well below the 10% threshold, no finding needed. PART B (consistency): Three factual contradictions found — Marcus's daughter is named 'Maddie' in Ch8 and 'Lily' in Ch11 (his ex-partner similarly changes from 'Julie' to 'Jen'), Viktor's blue shirt origin is stated as 'Day 1' in Ch10 and 'Day 8' in Ch12, and a spatial reference places Yuki 'three floors above Haruki' in a three-story building where the maximum separation is two floors. Timeline arithmetic, character availability after death, 14-day timer references, floor assignments, and object tracking are otherwise clean.

redundancy_findings.evidence: L57: 'Short black hair, sharp features, wiry shoulders. Suki Ota, red team.'; L122 (~65 lines later, same chapter): 'a woman with short black hair and sharp features is already halfway through lunch.' L156: 'Suki. Red team.' restates role a third time.
redundancy_findings.problem: Suki's physical description ('short black hair, sharp features') is established at L57 and repeated near-verbatim at L122 within the same chapter. Her role ('red team') is stated at L57 and restated at L156. A reader encounters the identical two-trait phrase twice in under 70 lines.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Trim L122 to the semicolon tattoo detail only (the sole new information); let the reader recognize Suki by the tattoo rather than re-reading her face. Cut the 'Suki. Red team.' at L156 if the preceding context already identifies her.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The phrases 'short black hair' and 'sharp features' appear only once in chapter 01. The red-team role is stated no more than twice (once in narration, once in dialogue if dramatically necessary).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch1 L7: 'Viktor Petrov is immediate: tall, thinning gray hair, glasses, the slight stoop...'; Ch2 L495: 'He's tall enough to be seen from anywhere in the room.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Viktor's tallness is established at Ch1 L7 and re-stated one chapter later at Ch2 L495. Adjacent-chapter re-description of the same physical trait.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Remove the height reference at L495. 'He's on his feet' or equivalent positional detail would suffice without reiterating tallness.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch2 does not describe Viktor as 'tall' or reference his height in any synonym.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch10 L2593: 'He is still in the blue button-down from Day 1, washed in a third-floor sink until the collar went gray. One cuff is frayed open and dark with old water.'; Ch12 L3212: 'The blue oxford he's worn since Day 8 has a dark collar and cuffs that are fraying where he's pulled at them.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Viktor's blue shirt with degraded collar and fraying cuffs is described in near-synonymous phrasing two chapters apart (Ch10 and Ch12). The shirt also changes origin day ('Day 1' vs 'Day 8'), which is a continuity error (see CON-003).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or sharply compress the shirt description at Ch12 L3212. If the shirt is mentioned at all, reference only what has changed since Ch10 (e.g., new stains). Reconcile the day reference to 'Day 1' to match Ch10.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12 does not re-describe the collar color or cuff fraying. Any shirt reference notes only what is new since Ch10.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch4 L979: 'a sheen on his forehead and a restless quality to his legs'; Ch5 L1168: 'Sweat shines across his forehead.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Marcus's forehead sweat/sheen is described in adjacent chapters (Ch4 and Ch5) with near-identical phrasing. The reader already registered this withdrawal symptom one chapter earlier.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace the forehead-sweat descriptor at Ch5 L1168 with a different withdrawal symptom or cut it entirely since the trembling hand in the same line already signals his state.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch5 does not describe sweat or sheen on Marcus's forehead.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 L3969: 'The blue strap of his runner's watch flashes when he lifts it.'; Ch15 L4159 (~190 lines later): 'The blue strap of his watch shows bright against the cuff of a gray sweater.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Jae-won's blue-strap runner's watch is described twice within Ch15 in near-identical phrasing. First establishment at L3969; redundant repetition at L4159.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the watch descriptor at L4159. If Jae-won needs identifying, use a different detail (sweater, posture, name).
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The blue-strap watch is described no more than once in chapter 15.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 L3969: 'The blue strap of his runner's watch flashes when he lifts it.'; Ch16 L4476: 'His blue-strap runner's watch glows whenever his sleeve slips back.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Jae-won's blue-strap runner's watch is described for the third time at Ch16 L4476, one chapter after two mentions in Ch15. The visual is already established.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L4476, reference the watch functionally (checking it, noting the time) rather than re-describing its color and strap.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 does not re-describe the watch's blue strap or appearance. Any watch reference is functional (time-checking).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 L3969 (first): 'The blue strap of his runner's watch flashes when he lifts it.'; Ch17 L4627 (fourth): 'A bulky runner's watch with a blue rubber strap rides his wrist.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Jae-won's watch is re-described for the fourth time across three chapters (Ch15 x2, Ch16 x1, Ch17 x1). This is the most over-described single prop in the manuscript.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L4627, drop the physical description entirely. Use 'his watch' — the reader has internalized the blue-strap image.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 does not describe the watch's color, strap material, or size.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch14 L3764: 'rice with separate grains, chicken in brown sauce, and a bottle of juice.'; Ch14 L3898 (~134 lines later): 'chicken in brown sauce and a bottle of juice.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Tomas's thermal-container meal is itemized twice within Ch14 using near-identical ingredient lists. Established at L3764.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut the itemized meal description at L3898. Reference the container or steam without re-listing contents.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The specific meal items (chicken in brown sauce, juice) are itemized no more than once in chapter 14.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch14 L3764: 'rice with separate grains, chicken in brown sauce, and a bottle of juice'; Ch15 L3973: 'the rice still holds separate grains, the chicken sits in brown sauce... A little bottle of orange juice sweats beside his elbow.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Tomas's meal is described for the third time at Ch15 L3973, in near-verbatim cross-chapter repetition from Ch14. 'Rice with separate grains,' 'chicken in brown sauce,' and 'bottle of juice' appear in substantially the same form across the chapter boundary.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L3973, skip the meal inventory. Show Tomas's privilege through a different channel (others' reactions, the smell, the warmth) rather than re-listing ingredients.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch15 does not itemize Tomas's meal contents.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Lena's cafeteria/first-floor control re-stated in Ch7 L1664, Ch8 L2059, Ch9 L2280, Ch10 L2581, Ch11 L3055, Ch12 L3236, Ch14 L3680, Ch15 L3975, Ch16 L4478; Ch17 L4635: 'That territory has been hers since the lines hardened.'
redundancy_findings.problem: By Ch17, Lena's territorial control has been stated in every chapter since Ch7 (10+ prior instances). L4635 adds no new information, angle, or consequence.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut L4635. Enter the scene through action or changed conditions rather than restating the power structure.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch17 does not summarize Lena's territorial control in exposition.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1942: 'Wire rims, the same pair from every conference photo, now repaired with a strip of surgical tape at the hinge.'; Ch11 L3013: 'The overhead light catches the lenses of his taped wire-rim glasses.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Chen Wei's taped wire-rim glasses are described at Ch7 L1942 and re-described in compressed but semantically identical form at Ch11 L3013.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L3013, reference 'his glasses' without the taped/wire-rim qualifier. The reader remembers the repair detail. The death-scene reference to the broken tape arm at L3133 may remain.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 does not re-describe the glasses as 'taped' or 'wire-rim' in the entry passage.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1664-1670: Pasternack at kitchen entrance, Mei-Ling at dock corridor; Ch7 L1752: 'Pasternack works the narrow point by the kitchen pass-through. Mei-Ling still has the dock corridor.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The cafeteria guard positions are inventoried at L1664 and re-inventoried 88 lines later at L1752 within the same chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At L1752, enter through what has changed since morning (queue lane, whiteboard) without re-listing guard positions. 'The same faces hold the same points' or similar compression.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The L1752 passage does not re-name Pasternack's or Mei-Ling's positions if unchanged since L1664.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1680: 'the sweet heavy rot coming off Stairwell A, four days of decomposition pushed through the vents'; Ch8 L2001: 'bile and sweat cutting through the sweet-rot that lives in every corridor now.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Decomposition smell described in consecutive chapter openings with the same compound modifier 'sweet rot'/'sweet heavy rot.' Ch8 re-teaches the olfactory state using near-identical vocabulary.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch8 L2001, enter through a different sensory channel or use a distinct smell descriptor. Do not use 'sweet' to modify decomposition.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 opening does not use 'sweet' to modify decomposition smell.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 L2953: 'The building has been cooling for three days, so gradually that her body keeps recognizing it before her mind does.'; Ch12 L3162: 'on the morning of the eleventh day the building is only cold enough to make every metal surface a small shock against bare skin.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Both consecutive chapter openings describe the building's progressive cooling as a body-registered sensory experience. The underlying information is identical.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch12 L3162, skip the general 'the building is cold' framing. Open with the specific new detail (metal surfaces as shocks) without the explanatory setup recapping Ch11's cooling narrative.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12 opening does not re-explain that the building has been gradually cooling.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch14 L3678: 'old rot from the stairwells, and the bathroom ammonia Lena's people have started using on everything'; Ch15 L3961: 'The cafeteria smells like bleach poured over rot.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Consecutive chapter openings describe the cafeteria smell with the same formula: rot + cleaning chemical.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch15 L3961, open through a different sensory channel. The reader has the rot-plus-chemical smell from Ch14.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch15 opening does not describe the smell as a combination of rot and cleaning chemicals.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch15 L3971: 'The building is thirty-eight degrees now'; Ch16 L4310: 'At thirty-eight degrees it feels salvaged from another world'; Ch16 L4362: 'The building is thirty-eight degrees.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The exact temperature 'thirty-eight degrees' appears in both consecutive chapter openings and a total of 4 times across Ch15-Ch16 (~500 lines). L4362 is a flat restatement.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch16, remove the explicit temperature number. Show the cold through its physical effects. Cut L4362 entirely; keep the mug detail at L4310 and the breath-fog detail at L4450.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch16 does not state the temperature as 'thirty-eight degrees' or repeat any specific number from Ch15.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch9 L2356: 'The surface is cold, colder than yesterday. The building has been cooling since sometime on Day 7'; Ch10 L2940: 'The keys are colder than yesterday. The building keeps cooling around the bodies.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Temperature/cold baseline re-proved at Ch10 L2940 without materially new consequence. Part of a four-chapter cluster (Ch10-13) that re-proves 'the building is getting colder' through incidental physical details (cold keys, cold floors, cold fabric) without new plot consequence, data, or mechanism. The cold baseline was analytically established in Ch9 via Suki's temperature analysis.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut or compress the cold reference at L2940. Reserve cold language for the Ch14 escalation (Priya's graph) and Ch15 frost/ice imagery.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch10 does not describe objects as 'colder than yesterday' or restate the progressive cooling trend.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 L3392: 'Air pushes through the ceiling vent, colder than it was this morning'; Ch13 L3549: 'Cold has pushed through the fabric at her knees'; Ch13 L3651: 'Cold from the floor works through Noor's shoes.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 contains two incidental cold re-proofs (L3549, L3651) that add no new information about the temperature drop. Part of the Ch10-13 cluster (7 cold re-proofs across 4 chapters without material escalation).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut one or both cold references. The cold is ambient by Ch13; let it live in the reader's assumption rather than re-proving it through fabric and shoes.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 contains at most one brief cold reference, not two separate re-proofs.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch12 L3168: 'his skin has the gray-yellow of a person whose organs are shutting down by committee'; Ch13 L3662: 'The skin around his eyes has gone the bruised yellow-gray of old fruit.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Marcus's gray-yellow skin is re-described in adjacent chapters with near-synonymous compound color descriptors. The 'gray-yellow'/'yellow-gray' phrasing repeats the same deterioration signal without new medical information. This is the third time since Ch8 L2007 ('His skin has gone gray').
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch13 L3662, describe Marcus through a different physical channel (sound of breathing, posture, smell) rather than repeating the skin-color observation.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch13 does not describe Marcus's skin color using any gray/yellow compound.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch3 L820: 'Noor counts because not counting would be worse'; Ch10 L2556: 'Noor counts because she always counts'; Ch11 L3131: 'Noor stands in the stairwell and counts because counting is more useful than deciding what the right number of seconds is.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The construction 'Noor counts because [rationale]' appears three times across the manuscript. Each offers a different psychological framing but re-proves the same baseline: Noor processes trauma through counting. The Ch10 version (L2556, 'It used to be Suki's job') carries grief and is the strongest. The Ch11 version (L3131) is the most expendable.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch11 L3131, have Noor count without the explanatory 'because' clause. Let the action stand alone by this point.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch11 does not include a 'Noor counts because...' construction.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch10 L2934: 'families keep sending messages through the relay about gardens, chemo, school plays, dinners they expect these people to come home and eat'; Ch11 L3007: 'Her mother's tomatoes are coming in. Priya's family is counting chemo rounds. Lily is rehearsing in the kitchen...'; Ch11 L3153: 'their families are still writing about tomato sauce, school plays, chemotherapy, transfer deadlines, restaurant openings.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The family-relay-message litany (gardens/tomatoes, chemo, school plays) is enumerated in near-identical list form in Ch10 (L2934), Ch11 (L3007), and Ch11 (L3153). Three versions of the same thematic catalogue in adjacent chapters, with two in the same chapter.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Keep the strongest version (Ch11 L3007, which individualizes each family). Cut the list at L3153 entirely — it recaps L3007 within the same chapter. At Ch10 L2934, either cut or differentiate so the litany is not pre-empted before its best rendering in Ch11.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: The gardens/chemo/school-plays litany appears in fully itemized form no more than once across Ch10-Ch11.

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch11 L3007: 'Her mother's tomatoes... Priya's family is counting chemo rounds. Lily is rehearsing...'; Ch12 L3164: 'Her mother's garden. Priya's family updates. Marcus's daughter's school play. The living world six inches of screen away and completely oblivious.'
redundancy_findings.problem: The family relay litany is re-enumerated at Ch12 L3164, one chapter after Ch11's two versions. The same three families (Noor's mother, Priya's family chemo, Marcus's daughter's play) appear in the same order.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch12 L3164, replace the itemized family list with a single compressed reference ('the relay messages keep arriving') or skip it entirely.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch12 does not re-enumerate the specific family relay topics (mother's garden, chemo, school play).

redundancy_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1996: 'Twenty-two living people. Eight dead. One sealed air supply carrying all of them.'; Ch8 L2001: 'Eight dead in six days, spread across three floors and two stairwells, and the HVAC carries all of them.'
redundancy_findings.problem: Chapter-seam recap: 'Eight dead' and 'carrying all of them' are near-verbatim across the Ch7/Ch8 boundary. The death count and the HVAC-carrying-the-dead image are restated within ~5 lines of the chapter break.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: At Ch8 L2001, cut 'Eight dead in six days' — the reader just encountered this tally. Enter Ch8 through the sensory detail (bile and sweat) without re-counting.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: Ch8 opening does not restate the death count from Ch7's closing line.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'corridor' appears 14 times in Ch14 (L3656-3958, ~302 lines). Manuscript-wide total: 159 occurrences across 18 chapters.
redundancy_findings.problem: The word 'corridor' appears 159 times in the manuscript (one every ~15 prose lines). Ch14 is tied for the worst chapter with 14 occurrences (~one per 21 lines). For an image appearing 20+ times, the target is retaining at most 8-10 manuscript-wide. At 159, approximately 90% need replacement or cutting.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch14, replace at least 8 of the 14 'corridor' instances with alternatives: hallway, hall, passage, the stretch between, the east run, or restructure sentences to avoid the spatial noun entirely.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'corridor' appears no more than 6 times in chapter 14.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'corridor' appears 14 times in Ch15 (L3959-4299, ~340 lines). Manuscript-wide total: 159.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch15 ties Ch14 as the worst corridor-density chapter. The word has become the manuscript's default spatial connector.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: In Ch15, replace at least 8 of the 14 instances with synonyms or sentence restructuring.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'corridor' appears no more than 6 times in chapter 15.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'corridor' appears 12 times in Ch12 (L3160-3396, ~236 lines = one per 20 lines). Manuscript-wide total: 159.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch12 has the third-highest corridor density. In the loading-dock assault chapter, the word occurs once every ~20 lines.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace at least 6 of the 12 instances. During the assault sequence, use directional cues ('ahead,' 'the turn,' 'the stretch past the server room') instead of 'corridor' for spatial orientation.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'corridor' appears no more than 6 times in chapter 12.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'blood' appears 17 times in Ch17 (L4623-4795, ~172 lines = one per 10 lines). Manuscript-wide total: 155.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch17 has the highest blood density in the manuscript, tied with Ch2. In the 172-line endgame chapter, the word averages once every 10 lines. While violence is central, many instances could use sensory alternatives.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace at least 8 of the 17 'blood' instances with alternatives: the wet, the spreading dark, copper, the slick, warmth on skin. Keep 'blood' for the most impactful moments.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'blood' appears no more than 9 times in chapter 17.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'terminal' appears 16 times in Ch6 (L1337-1659, ~322 lines); 'relay' appears 26 times in Ch6. Combined: 42 tech-noun occurrences in one chapter.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch6 is the Tomas/relay-manipulation chapter. 'Terminal' (16x) and 'relay' (26x) together create extreme lexical monotony — 42 tech nouns in 322 lines, roughly one every 8 lines. Manuscript-wide: 'terminal' 131x, 'relay' 66x.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace at least 10 'relay' instances with 'the message system,' 'the family channel,' 'the inbound feed.' Replace at least 8 'terminal' instances with 'screen,' 'monitor,' 'workstation,' 'the machine.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'relay' appears no more than 16 times and 'terminal' no more than 8 times in chapter 06.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'stairwell' appears 15 times in Ch7 (L1660-1998, ~338 lines = one per 22 lines). Manuscript-wide total: 108.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch7 has the highest stairwell density. The word averages once every 22 lines. Alternatives exist: stairs, the landing, the A-side steps, the east flight.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace at least 7 of the 15 instances with synonyms.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'stairwell' appears no more than 8 times in chapter 07.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'blanket' appears 16 times in Ch15 (L3959-4299, ~340 lines = one per 21 lines). 27 of 40 manuscript-wide occurrences (67%) are in Ch15-Ch16.
redundancy_findings.problem: The word 'blanket' is extremely concentrated in Ch15, appearing once every ~21 lines during the resource-stripping and death scenes.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Replace at least 8 of the 16 instances. Use alternatives: the thermal wrap, the covering, by pronoun ('it'), or restructure sentences to avoid the noun.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'blanket' appears no more than 8 times in chapter 15.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'The' opens 56 of 194 sentences in Ch18 (28.9%). Manuscript-wide: 'The' opens 1,015 of 6,461 sentences (15.7%, numerator 1015, denominator 6461). Every chapter exceeds the 7% threshold.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch18 has the worst 'The'-opener density at 28.9% — nearly one in three sentences begins with 'The.' This is the manuscript's most pervasive sentence-pattern monotony issue. The chapter's short length (194 sentences) makes the pattern especially noticeable.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite at least 25 of the 56 'The'-initial sentences to open with different constructions: verbs, adverbs, prepositional phrases, character names, subordinate clauses. Target density below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: No more than 29 sentences in chapter 18 begin with 'The' (~15% of 194).

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'The' opens 72 of 366 sentences in Ch15 (19.7%). Manuscript-wide: 1,015/6,461 = 15.7%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch15 has the second-worst density at 19.7%. Nearly one in five sentences begins with 'The.'
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite at least 17 of the 72 'The'-initial sentences to use varied constructions. Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: No more than 55 sentences in chapter 15 begin with 'The' (~15% of 366).

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'The' opens 69 of 383 sentences in Ch3 (18.0%). Manuscript-wide: 1,015/6,461 = 15.7%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch3 has the third-worst 'The'-opener density at 18.0%, materially above the already-high manuscript average.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite at least 12 of the 69 'The'-initial sentences. Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: No more than 57 sentences in chapter 03 begin with 'The.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'The' opens 60 of 335 sentences in Ch5 (17.9%). Manuscript-wide: 1,015/6,461 = 15.7%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch5 has 17.9% 'The'-opener density, materially above the manuscript average.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite at least 10 of the 60 'The'-initial sentences. Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: No more than 50 sentences in chapter 05 begin with 'The.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'The' opens 73 of 411 sentences in Ch2 (17.8%). Manuscript-wide: 1,015/6,461 = 15.7%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch2 has 17.8% 'The'-opener density.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite at least 11 of the 73 'The'-initial sentences. Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: No more than 62 sentences in chapter 02 begin with 'The.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'The' opens 63 of 356 sentences in Ch12 (17.7%). Manuscript-wide: 1,015/6,461 = 15.7%.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch12 has 17.7% 'The'-opener density.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite at least 10 of the 63 'The'-initial sentences. Target below 15%.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: No more than 53 sentences in chapter 12 begin with 'The.'

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'still' appears ~22 times in Ch13 (~3,470 prose words = 1 per 158 words). Manuscript-wide: 321 occurrences / 75,542 prose words = 1 per 235 words, which is 3.4x the 1/800 threshold.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch13 has the worst 'still' density in the manuscript (1 per 158 words, 5.1x threshold). 'Still' is the single most overused qualifier: 321 total occurrences. It creates a pervasive verbal tic suggesting stasis/continuation where the prose should convey forward motion.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 12 of the ~22 'still' instances in Ch13. In most cases, the sentence works without the word: 'the server room is locked' instead of 'the server room is still locked.' Where temporal persistence genuinely matters, replace with 'yet,' 'continues,' or restructure.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'still' appears no more than 10 times in chapter 13.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'still' appears ~20 times in Ch5 (~3,260 prose words = 1 per 163 words, 4.9x threshold). Manuscript-wide: 321/75,542.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch5 has the second-worst 'still' density (1 per 163 words).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 10 of the ~20 instances. Most can be deleted without altering meaning.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'still' appears no more than 10 times in chapter 05.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'still' appears ~24 times in Ch7 (~3,930 prose words = 1 per 164 words, 4.9x threshold). Manuscript-wide: 321/75,542.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch7 has the third-worst 'still' density (1 per 164 words).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 12 of the ~24 instances.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'still' appears no more than 12 times in chapter 07.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'still' appears ~22 times in Ch9 (~3,760 prose words = 1 per 171 words, 4.7x threshold). Manuscript-wide: 321/75,542.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch9 has the fourth-worst 'still' density (1 per 171 words).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 10 of the ~22 instances.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'still' appears no more than 12 times in chapter 09.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'still' appears ~15 times in Ch3 (~2,970 prose words = 1 per 198 words, 4.0x threshold). 'already' appears ~9 times (1 per 330 words, 2.4x threshold). Manuscript-wide: 'still' 321/75,542; 'already' 148/75,542.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch3 has high density for both flagged hedge words. The combined effect creates a prose pattern of over-signaling temporal state ('still locked,' 'already dead,' 'still sealed,' 'already cold').
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 8 'still' and 4 'already' instances. Delete the word where the sentence works without it.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'still' appears no more than 7 times and 'already' no more than 5 times in chapter 03.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'already' appears ~15 times in Ch4 (~4,400 prose words = 1 per 293 words, 2.7x threshold). Manuscript-wide: 148/75,542 = 1 per 510 words.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch4 has the worst 'already' density (1 per 293 words, 2.7x the 1/800 threshold). The word over-signals that events have progressed, undermining forward momentum.
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 8 of the ~15 instances. 'Already' is almost always deletable without loss: 'the corridor smells of blood' works as well as 'the corridor already smells of blood.'
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'already' appears no more than 7 times in chapter 04.

redundancy_findings.evidence: 'already' appears ~14 times in Ch15 (~4,480 prose words = 1 per 320 words, 2.5x threshold). Manuscript-wide: 148/75,542.
redundancy_findings.problem: Ch15 has the second-worst 'already' density (1 per 320 words).
redundancy_findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 7 of the ~14 instances.
redundancy_findings.acceptance_test: 'already' appears no more than 7 times in chapter 15.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8 L2235: 'It's Julie... Maddie had a piano recital. Clementi.'; Ch8 L2239: 'She's seven. Maddie's seven.'; Ch11 L2969: 'Marcus has one from Jen. Lily's play is on the fifteenth. She got the narrator part.'
consistency_findings.problem: Marcus's daughter is named 'Maddie' (age seven, piano recital) in Ch8 and 'Lily' (school play, narrator part) in Ch11. His ex-partner is named 'Julie' in Ch8 and 'Jen' in Ch11. These are irreconcilable name changes for the same family members.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Unify Marcus's family names across the manuscript. Pick one daughter name (Maddie or Lily) and one ex-partner name (Julie or Jen) and correct all instances in the non-canonical chapter. If Ch8's names are canonical, correct Ch11 L2969 and L3007. If Ch11's names are canonical, correct Ch8 L2235, L2239, L2243, and L2255.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Marcus's daughter has exactly one name and his ex-partner has exactly one name across all chapters.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch8 L2235: 'It's Julie... Maddie had a piano recital.'; Ch11 L2969: 'Marcus has one from Jen. Lily's play is on the fifteenth.'
consistency_findings.problem: Mirror finding of CON-001: whichever chapter contains the incorrect names must be corrected. Ch8 is the first occurrence, so it establishes the baseline. If the error is in Ch8 rather than Ch11, this chapter needs correction.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: See CON-001. Whichever set of names is chosen as canonical, update the other chapter to match.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: Marcus's family names are identical in Ch8 and Ch11.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch10 L2593: 'He is still in the blue button-down from Day 1'; Ch12 L3212: 'The blue oxford he's worn since Day 8.'
consistency_findings.problem: Viktor's blue shirt is stated to have been worn 'from Day 1' in Ch10 and 'since Day 8' in Ch12. These are contradictory origin days for the same garment. 'Day 1' is more plausible (arrival clothing).
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change 'since Day 8' at Ch12 L3212 to 'since Day 1' or simply 'since the sprint began' to match Ch10.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: All references to Viktor's blue shirt cite a consistent origin day.

consistency_findings.evidence: Ch7 L1974: 'Yuki still stains the east stairwell three floors above Haruki.'; Ch4 L1001: Yuki is on 'the landing between the second and third floors'; Ch7 L1972: Haruki is on 'the first-floor landing of Stairwell A.'
consistency_findings.problem: In a three-story building, the maximum floor separation is two floors. Yuki (landing between Floors 2-3) and Haruki (Floor 1 landing) are at most two floors apart. 'Three floors above' is spatially impossible.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Change 'three floors above' to 'two floors above' or 'two landings above' at Ch7 L1974.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The spatial relationship between Yuki's and Haruki's positions is physically possible within a three-story building.

consistency_findings.evidence: Chapters 1-13 and 15: 'Tomas' with consistent accent. Ch14 lines 3764, 3768, 3770, 3900, 3936, 3946: 'Tomas' without accent (if applicable — encoding-dependent). The same chapter uses both forms.
consistency_findings.problem: The character name 'Tomas' alternates between accented and unaccented forms within Ch14, and possibly between chapters. This is likely an encoding artifact from the drafting process but creates inconsistency in the published text.
consistency_findings.rewrite_direction: Normalize all instances of the name to a single consistent form across the entire manuscript. Use find-and-replace to ensure uniformity.
consistency_findings.acceptance_test: The character name uses one consistent spelling/encoding in every occurrence.
